summary: Foreign bodies do not typically migrate directly from their initial site toward the heart; instead, they may penetrate surrounding tissues causing complications like mediastinal involvement but are extremely unlikely to enter cardiac muscle due to anatomical barriers and lack of documented cases supporting direct intracardiac migration. This makes it clinically improbable for a foreign object to travel systematically to the heart via passive movement after entering the GI tract.
